skill.name: bash-defensive-patterns
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code in writing production-ready Bash scripts with strict mode, safe file handling, argument validation, logging, cleanup traps, and dry-run support.
skill.summary: Apply defensive Bash patterns for safer automation scripts and CI/CD tasks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, filesystem, URL, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found these are Bash examples and documentation references, not executable skill code. No prompt injection, credential access, hidden network behavior, or malicious intent was found. Residual risk is low because generated Bash guidance can include destructive operations if users request them without safeguards.

low_findings.title: External Command Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static command execution findings occur in Markdown Bash examples that teach strict mode, command substitution, dependency checks, and shell script structure. These examples are not executed by the skill, but they can influence generated automation, so users should still review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside fenced Bash examples and surrounding instructional text. I found no runnable wrapper, installer, or hidden command path in SKILL.md or the playbook.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Operations Are Safety Guidance
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings point to examples for temporary directories, cleanup traps, atomic writes, and dry-run wrappers. They include potentially destructive commands such as remove operations, but the context presents defensive patterns rather than malicious file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly discuss safe cleanup, validation, dry-run behavior, and avoiding unsafe overwrites. The only residual concern is user misuse when adapting generated shell commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The network findings are documentation links to Bash and shell scripting resources. They do not fetch data, transmit secrets, or define network behavior inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a Resources section as human-readable references. No code in the reviewed files sends requests to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static high-severity weak cryptography findings do not match actual cryptographic operations in context. The referenced lines are ordinary Bash examples, comments, or headings, so there is no evidence of unsafe hashing, encryption, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited areas found copy, move, remove, ownership, and safety guidance rather than cryptographic APIs or algorithms. Confidence is slightly lower only because the static analyzer did not expose the exact matched token.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Process Cleanup Examples
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance findings point to process liveness checks and waits inside a signal cleanup pattern. This is normal defensive process management, not host inventory, stealth discovery, or data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section is named process orchestration with signals and only tracks child process identifiers. I found no commands that enumerate sensitive host, user, network, or credential information.

content.value_statement: Shell scripts often fail in production because they lack validation, cleanup, and clear error handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Bash patterns for safer automation.
actual_capabilities: Applies Bash strict mode with error and pipe failure handling.
Guides safe variable quoting, array handling, and command substitution.
Provides patterns for cleanup traps, temporary files, and atomic writes.
Builds argument parsing, logging, dry-run, and dependency checks.
Supports idempotent script design for repeatable automation workflows.
limitations: Targets Bash and does not provide strict POSIX sh portability.
Provides guidance and examples, not an automated security scanner.
Generated scripts still require review before use on production systems.
Does not verify platform-specific behavior across every Unix variant.

use_cases.title: Harden Deployment Scripts
use_cases.description: Add strict mode, cleanup traps, dry-run behavior, and validation to deployment automation.

use_cases.title: Improve CI/CD Shell Steps
use_cases.description: Rewrite fragile pipeline snippets with safer quoting, logging, and dependency checks.

use_cases.title: Create Maintainable Admin Utilities
use_cases.description: Design reusable Bash functions with clear errors, safe file handling, and predictable reruns.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Safe Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Bash script template for my task. Include strict mode, clear usage text, input validation, logging, cleanup, and a dry-run option.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Pipeline Step
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this CI shell step for defensive Bash issues. Focus on quoting, error handling, dependency checks, temporary files, and safe cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Existing Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Bash script using defensive patterns. Preserve behavior, add safer argument parsing, improve logging, and explain each risk reduced.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Reusable Shell Library
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a small Bash helper library for production automation. Include function conventions, error traps, atomic file writes, idempotent operations, and testing guidance.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a safer backup script template.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a structured Bash plan with strict mode, validated paths, dry-run support, timestamped logs, temporary workspace cleanup, and clear failure messages.

output_examples.input: A user provides a fragile CI command sequence.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies missing quoting, weak error handling, and unchecked dependencies, then describes a safer pipeline-ready rewrite.

output_examples.input: A user wants an idempotent setup utility.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes checks for existing directories and configuration files, guarded file writes, and repeatable behavior with no unintended overwrites.

best_practices: Confirm the target shell, operating system, and permissions before generating a script.
Use dry-run modes and explicit confirmations for destructive operations.
Test error paths, missing dependencies, empty inputs, and reruns before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not paste generated shell scripts into production terminals without review.
Do not hide destructive commands behind broad helper functions.
Do not assume Bash-specific syntax works in POSIX sh environments.

faq.question: Does this skill execute shell commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides Bash writing guidance and examples for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it make existing scripts safer?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review and refactor scripts for quoting, validation, logging, cleanup, and error handling.

faq.question: Is it suitable for POSIX sh scripts?
faq.answer: Not as the primary target. The guidance uses Bash features such as arrays and extended conditionals.

faq.question: Does it replace a shell security audit?
faq.answer: No. It improves defensive structure, but production scripts still need human review and testing.

faq.question: What workflows benefit most?
faq.answer: CI/CD jobs, deployment scripts, system administration utilities, and repeatable maintenance automation benefit most.

faq.question: How should I use destructive command examples?
faq.answer: Use dry-run mode, validate inputs, require confirmation, and review every filesystem target before execution.
